nativeRange.habitat: Found on inshore and offshore coral reefs, as well as in harbors and along protected outer reef slopes, at depths of roughly 5–30 m. The species has been documented forming loose shoals in areas with stronger water movement. An established invasive population has been reported in the Gulf of Mexico and Caribbean since around 2013.

diet.notes: In the wild, has been documented feeding primarily on zooplankton in midwater above the reef. In captivity, generally accepts a varied diet of mysis, brine shrimp, frozen marine preparations, and quality flake or pellet foods; small, frequent feedings are commonly advised to match its planktivorous natural feeding pattern.

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso, 4757x3171. Sharp, vibrant in-water lateral profile clearly showing all diagnostics: deeply forked tail with bright yellow tips, yellow on upper rear body, black blotch behind pectoral, and blue-speckled bluish body. Best ID and most striking shot.
Rickard Zerpe, 4166x3125. Sharp, colorful reef shot showing yellow upper-rear body, yellow-edged forked tail and dorsal, blue scale speckling; slightly downward angle for variety.
AravindManoj, 4702x3135. Clean full lateral studio shot on black background; sharp reference of the deeply forked yellow-tipped tail and body form. Slightly dark but excellent for ID.

summary: A small Indo-West Pacific damselfish recognized for its deeply forked tail. Generally considered one of the more peaceful damsels and is best kept in loose shoals with ample open swimming space over live rock.
careNotes:
Adults typically reach 8–10 cm and are silver-grey to bluish overall, with a black blotch above the pectoral fin, yellow on the upper rear of the body, and the deeply forked tail that gives the species its common name. Unlike many other damselfishes, the Scissortail Damselfish is generally considered relatively peaceful and is among the few members of the family that tend to do well when kept in groups of five or more. In the aquarium it is primarily a midwater swimmer, and the species benefits from a tank with a long footprint and plenty of unobstructed open water; live rock should be provided to give the fish shelter when stressed. A minimum of 300 L is generally advised to comfortably house a shoal.

Outside its native Indo-West Pacific range, the species has become established as an invasive fish in the Gulf of Mexico and parts of the Caribbean since around 2013, with populations documented on offshore oil platforms and natural reefs. The introduction pathway — suspected to be ballast water discharge or oil-platform fouling — has not been confirmed.
breedingNotes: Oviparous with distinct pairing reported. Males have been documented preparing and guarding adhesive demersal eggs on the substrate; eggs typically hatch in about 4–5 days and larvae are pelagic for roughly 17–20 days before settling. Has been bred in captivity (an ORA captive-bred line is commercially available), but routine hobbyist breeding is rarely reported.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary species page found via search after the collection-level URL did not surface the species directly. Care level (moderate→adjusted to beginner per consensus), tank (30 gal / ~113 L), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.020–1.025), dKH (8–12), max size (3.75 in / 9.5 cm), temperament ("semi-aggressive" / peaceful for a damsel), reef safe (yes), diet (herbivore — disagreement noted). Native range claim (Gulf of California to Peru) appears to be a misidentification with Chromis atrilobata and was rejected in favor of the Indo-West Pacific consensus.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Designated secondary. Confirmed scientific name (Bleeker, 1856), family Pomacentridae, max size 10.0 cm TL, depth range 5–30 m, Indo-West Pacific distribution (Persian Gulf, Red Sea, East Africa to Philippines, southern Japan, northern Australia, Melanesia), reef-associated diurnal planktivorous biology, oviparous reproduction with male egg-guarding, IUCN Least Concern. Note: the originally supplied secondary URL pointed at Chromis atripectoralis (a different species) — corrected to the Neopomacentrus-cyanomos page.

additional.name: Tropical Fish Keeping
additional.notes: Cross-checked native range (Indo-West Pacific + Gulf of Mexico invasion since 2013), tank size (55 gal / ~208 L), water parameters (72–78 °F, pH 8.0–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, 8–12 dKH), lifespan (~5 yr), omnivorous diet, schooling behavior in groups of 6–9+, and breeding (male guards adhesive eggs, ~4–5 day hatch, 17–20 day pelagic larval phase). Used in numerical averaging.

additional.name: Reeflex
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (8–10 cm), temperature (22–25 °C / 71.6–77 °F), Indo-Pacific distribution, schooling requirement, and an outlier minimum tank recommendation of 1000 L. Reef-safety and temperament ratings disagree with most other sources; the more common ratings were adopted.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Taxonomy/range fallback. Confirmed Indo-Pacific native range, Gulf of Mexico / Yucatan invasion, family Pomacentridae, and IUCN Least Concern (2022 assessment).

additional.name: fishy.wiki
additional.notes: Cross-checked max size (9.4 cm), tank minimum (113 L), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.020–1.025), dKH (8–12), and care level (moderate, beginner-suitable). Native range listed appears mistakenly copied from Chromis atrilobata and was not used.

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous with male nest-guarding. Captive breeding has been achieved commercially (e.g., ORA captive-bred line) but is not commonly reported among hobbyists.
